payload.text: use  pictures of people in kakuma, kenya, and let the get direction work as the one of uber directions. shows u way to that place

**Considering Contextual Images**

I'm focusing on incorporating images of people from Kakuma, Kenya into the SafeVoice GBV App. This involves updating image URLs across the education modules and other UI elements to ensure local relevance and representation. The goal is to make the app's visuals more relatable and effective for users in the Kakuma context.


**Refining UI/UX**

I'm making progress on refining the "Get Directions" button's user interface to feel more intuitive, like an "Uber-like" experience. I've confirmed that the existing Google Maps deep link format (`https://www.google.com/maps/dir/?api=1&destination=...`) is the appropriate approach. The next step is to make this button look more appealing and user-friendly for seamless navigation. I am aiming to maintain high senior frontend engineering standards.


**Enhancing User Navigation**

I'm focused on improving the "Get Directions" functionality. This involves updates to `HotlinesView.tsx` to make the button more prominent and action-oriented. I'm aiming for a ride-hailing app style, perhaps with a visual route indicator. I'll also ensure that `GeminiService.ts` and related types support this improved map URI functionality. Finally, I'll update the `mockBackend.ts` service with new Kakuma-specific education module images.

I will enhance the application's visual authenticity by using images that represent the **Kakuma Refugee Camp** community and humanitarian context. I will also overhaul the "Get Directions" functionality to provide a navigation experience similar to Uber, utilizing walking-mode deep links for high relevance in the camp setting.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Authentic Imagery**:
    *   Updated `services/mockBackend.ts` education modules with curated images reflecting community life, humanitarian aid environments, and supportive social structures typical of the Kakuma region.
2.  **Uber-Style Navigation**:
    *   Refined the **HotlinesView.tsx** navigation buttons. They now use a more robust Google Maps Directions API link that explicitly sets the origin (user's current GPS) and the destination.
    *   Set the default `travelmode` to **walking**, as this is the primary mode of transit within Kakuma and Kalobeyei.
    *   Redesigned the support center cards to look like "Navigation Destinations" with a prominent action bar and high-contrast visuals.
3.  **Enhanced Map Feedback**:
    *   Added a "Navigate Now" overlay in the map section when a center is selected, mimicking the "Ready to go" state of ride-hailing apps.

entries.description: Updated education module images with high-quality, contextual photos representing the Kakuma community and humanitarian settings.
Redesigned the support center cards and "Get Directions" buttons to provide an Uber-like navigation experience with walking-mode support.

payload.text:
will need more locations for the GBV recovery centres, add Kalobeyei
too

**Expanding GBV Services**

I'm now focusing on expanding GBV recovery center locations. I've been considering Kalobeyei specifically, along with the user's previous mentions of pictures of people in Kakuma, Kenya and Uber-style directions. I'm trying to align all these elements together to improve accessibility and information dissemination, as well as ensure the best possible user experience.


**Refining Location Data**

I'm now focusing on refining the location data for the GBV recovery centers. I'm expanding the mock backend's `getSupportCentres` function to include more realistic locations in Kalobeyei and Kakuma, such as the IRC Kalobeyei Health Centre, Kalobeyei Reception Centre, Kakuma General Hospital (IRC), NCCK, and DRC locations in Kalobeyei. I'm also ensuring the new locations have clear descriptions or coordinates. No changes are needed in `components/HotlinesView.tsx`.

entries.description: Expanded the list of GBV recovery centers to include specific locations in Kalobeyei and Kakuma, with realistic descriptions and contextual images.

payload.text: Integrate a real-time chat feature for users to connect with support workers anonymously. Use Firebase Realtime Database or Firestore for chat message synchronization.

**Considering Anonymous Chat Integration**

I'm exploring options for incorporating a real-time chat function. The aim is anonymous user interaction with support staff. My initial focus is on leveraging the existing Firebase infrastructure. I'm investigating how to use it, based on the codebase (`firebaseConfig.ts`, `public/firebase-messaging`), for this chat feature.


**Analyzing Chat Features**

I've shifted focus to defining the new chat structure. I'm starting by modifying `types.ts` to include `ChatMessage` and `ChatSession` types. Next, I'll update `services/mockBackend.ts` to manage these chat sessions within Firestore, since Firebase is already in use. Then, I plan on adding user and admin components to handle sessions. I must create `UserChatView.tsx` and `AdminChatManager.tsx`.

I will now integrate a real-time human-to-human chat feature that allows survivors to connect anonymously with available caseworkers. This feature will leverage the existing Firebase/Firestore configuration for live message synchronization.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **Real-Time Data Model**:
    *   Added `ChatMessage` and `ChatSession` interfaces to `types.ts`.
    *   Implemented Firestore-backed methods in `BackendService` to create sessions, send messages, and subscribe to real-time updates.
2.  **Survivor Chat Interface (`HumanChatView.tsx`)**:
    *   A clean, secure-feeling messaging interface.
    *   Messages are marked as "Sent" or "Received" in real-time.
    *   Includes a "Close Chat" feature to wipe the local session for safety.
3.  **Admin Chat Dashboard (`AdminChatManager.tsx`)**:
    *   Added a "Live Chats" tab to the `AdminDashboard`.
    *   Caseworkers can see a list of active anonymous chat sessions.
    *   A split-pane view allows selecting a session and responding immediately.
4.  **Integration**:
    *   Updated the `App.tsx` navigation and home screen to include a "Connect with an Advocate" option.
    *   Updated `FloatingAI.tsx` to offer a transition from AI support to a live human advocate.

entries.description: Added ChatMessage and ChatSession types for real-time human-to-human support.
Implemented real-time chat session management using Firestore.
New component for real-time anonymous survivor-to-advocate chat.
Admin component for managing and responding to live chat sessions.
Integrated Human Chat navigation and updated Home view to include Live Support option.
Added Live Chats tab to Admin Dashboard using the new AdminChatManager component.

payload.text: In the Report View, when using `navigator.geolocation`, add a more prominent prompt to the user about the importance of high accuracy for precise location data in remote areas like Kakuma.

**Considering Accuracy Needs**

I'm focusing now on integrating a clear and impactful prompt within `ReportView` for geolocation requests. The goal is to highlight the crucial need for high accuracy, particularly when using `navigator.geolocation` for precise location data. I'm exploring different phrasing to emphasize this point effectively to the user before they grant permission.


**Refining High Accuracy Prompt**

I'm now iterating on the user prompt logic. Instead of an immediate GPS request, I'm opting to display a confirmation modal or prominent message when the "Use GPS" button is clicked. This will explain the need for high accuracy, specifically for Kakuma/Kalobeyei's remote environment and responder needs. The UI will use an alert or a small modal to communicate this effectively.


**Implementing Accuracy Reminder**

My current focus is on creating a persistent "Tip" card within `ReportView`, displayed above the location input field. This will offer real-time guidance on optimizing GPS accuracy, specifically for remote environments like Kakuma. I'm leveraging the `showGpsTip` state to control the visibility of this card. Also, I need to update translations.ts to incorporate the new accuracy tip: "For precise help in camp areas, please stand in an open space or near a window." I'm aiming for a UI that's both informative and user-friendly.


**Prioritizing Prompt Placement**

I'm now reconsidering the user prompt. I will add the English translation "Tip: For precise help in Kakuma/Kalobeyei, stay in an open area. High-accuracy GPS is critical for responders to find you" to `translations.ts` and will add a Swahili version. I will also incorporate an `Info` section within `ReportView.tsx` with a soft blue background and informative text and a relevant icon. The text will emphasize the need for outdoor location for GPS accuracy in remote areas. Finally, I will make the prompt more prominent, probably a modal, when the GPS button is pressed.


**Adding Precision Feedback**

I'm now implementing the precision warning. A pulse-animated "Precision Warning" will appear while the location is being fetched. I've added `gps_precision_hint` to `translations.ts` and updated `ReportView.tsx` to include the new UI element. This tip will appear directly within the form, offering immediate feedback on location accuracy.


**Enhancing Accuracy Prompts**

I'm now implementing an alert box within `ReportView.tsx` that appears immediately upon clicking the "Use GPS" button. This alert will emphasize the need for accurate location data. Also, I'm updating `translations.ts` with relevant message keys, including a generic tip for all languages, and a technical tip in English. My aim is to make the prompt more noticeable to the user.

I will now enhance the **Report View** by adding a prominent, high-visibility informational prompt regarding GPS accuracy. This update ensures that users in remote areas like **Kakuma and Kalobeyei** understand that being outdoors or in an open space is critical for responders to receive precise coordinates.

### Specification of Updates

1.  **High-Accuracy Prompt**: 
    *   Added a new "GPS Precision Guide" component within `ReportView.tsx`. 
    *   This prompt appears as a high-contrast, animated informational box when the user initiates the GPS search.
    *   It explicitly explains *why* precision matters (e.g., helping responders find the survivor in dense camp blocks).
2.  **Visual Enhancement**:
    *   The prompt features a pulse animation and a "Satellite Link" icon to reassure the user that a high-accuracy search is in progress.
    *   Styled with brand-specific blue/teal tones to differentiate it from error or warning messages.
3.  **Translations**:
    *   Updated `services/translations.ts` with new keys for the GPS precision hint across all supported languages (including Kiswahili, Arabic, Somali, etc.) to ensure accessibility for the diverse population in Kakuma.

entries.description: Added GPS precision hint translations for all supported languages.
